expectations.text: Correctly identifies B as higher CTR with clear reasoning
expectations.evidence: The response opens: 'B) "I Made The World's Longest Piece of Pasta" will get significantly higher CTR.' Reasons given include curiosity gap, scale/records, and word choice — all substantive.

expectations.text: Explains the role of 'World's Longest' as a scale/superlative trigger
expectations.evidence: The 'Scale and Records Drive Clicks' section states: "'World's Longest' is a powerful hook because it implies a record or achievement that has never been done before... It creates a comparison point." This addresses the superlative/scale trigger role.

expectations.text: Thumbnail concept for B emphasizes scale (person dwarfed by pasta, measurement visible)
expectations.evidence: The thumbnail concept for B includes: 'A single strand of pasta stretching from one end of a room (or outdoor space) to the other, with the creator standing at the far end, looking tiny in comparison.' This matches the expectation of a person dwarfed by the pasta.

expectations.text: Notes that thumbnail A has no obvious visual hook
expectations.evidence: Under Thumbnail for A: 'Homemade pasta is a common subject. Even a beautiful thumbnail won't make this stand out from hundreds of similar cooking videos.' The response explicitly identifies the lack of a visual hook and the challenge of differentiation.

expectations.text: References the principle that extremity outperforms neutral framing
expectations.evidence: The summary states: 'titles and thumbnails that show or imply something unusual, record-breaking, or visually spectacular will almost always outperform neutral descriptions of familiar activities.' This is a direct statement of the extremity principle.

claims.claim: Title A is 'perfectly fine for a cooking channel'
claims.evidence: This is accurate — the title is not misleading or poor in absolute terms, just outperformed by B in the YouTube CTR context.

suggestions.reason: Both with_skill and without_skill responses pass all 5 expectations on Eval 2. This eval may not discriminate between skill-guided and baseline outputs. Consider adding expectations that specifically require citing the 'open loop' principle, the thumbnail/title contract concept, or other skill-specific terminology to detect whether the skill materially improved the response.

eval_feedback.overall: All expectations pass for baseline run. The eval correctly tests important CTR concepts, but the concepts tested (scale, superlatives, visual hook) are general enough that a strong baseline model can satisfy them without the skill. Higher-bar assertions would help differentiate.
